warnings.desc: Version of Lynis is very old and should be updated
No password set for single mode
Found one or more vulnerable packages.
Found some information disclosure in SMTP banner (OS or software name)
iptables module(s) loaded, but no rules active

suggestions.desc: Install libpam-tmpdir to set $TMP and $TMPDIR for PAM sessions
Install libpam-usb to enable multi-factor authentication for PAM sessions
Install apt-listbugs to display a list of critical bugs prior to each APT installation.
Install apt-listchanges to display any significant changes prior to any upgrade via APT.
Install debian-goodies so that you can run checkrestart after upgrades to determine which services are using old versions of libraries and need restarting.
Install needrestart, alternatively to debian-goodies, so that you can run needrestart after upgrades to determine which daemons are using old versions of libraries and need restarting.
Install debsecan to generate lists of vulnerabilities which affect this installation.
Install debsums for the verification of installed package files against MD5 checksums.
Install fail2ban to automatically ban hosts that commit multiple authentication errors.
Set a password on GRUB bootloader to prevent altering boot configuration (e.g. boot in single user mode without password)
Protect rescue.service by using sulogin
Determine why /vmlinuz is missing on this Debian/Ubuntu system.
Check the output of apt-cache policy manually to determine why output is empty
Install a PAM module for password strength testing like pam_cracklib or pam_passwdqc
Configure minimum password age in /etc/login.defs
Configure maximum password age in /etc/login.defs
Set password for single user mode to minimize physical access attack surface
Default umask in /etc/login.defs could be more strict like 027
To decrease the impact of a full /home file system, place /home on a separated partition
To decrease the impact of a full /tmp file system, place /tmp on a separated partition
To decrease the impact of a full /var file system, place /var on a separated partition
Disable drivers like USB storage when not used, to prevent unauthorized storage or data theft
Add the IP name and FQDN to /etc/hosts for proper name resolving
Purge old/removed packages (2 found) with aptitude purge or dpkg --purge command. This will cleanup old configuration files, cron jobs and startup scripts.
Install debsums utility for the verification of packages with known good database.
Update your system with apt-get update, apt-get upgrade, apt-get dist-upgrade and/or unattended-upgrades
Install package apt-show-versions for patch management purposes
Check your resolv.conf file and fill in a backup nameserver if possible
Consider running ARP monitoring software (arpwatch,arpon)
You are advised to hide the mail_name (option: smtpd_banner) from your postfix configuration. Use postconf -e or change your main.cf file (/etc/postfix/main.cf)
Disable the 'VRFY' command
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Consider hardening SSH configuration
Check what deleted files are still in use and why.
Add a legal banner to /etc/issue, to warn unauthorized users
Add legal banner to /etc/issue.net, to warn unauthorized users
Enable process accounting
Enable sysstat to collect accounting (no results)
Enable auditd to collect audit information
Install a file integrity tool to monitor changes to critical and sensitive files
One or more sysctl values differ from the scan profile and could be tweaked
Harden compilers like restricting access to root user only
Harden the system by installing at least one malware scanner, to perform periodic file system scans
